ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302757 covers 'meatball' as a general category. No specific Indonesian bakso (beef meatball) entry exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers processed beef meat products. No specific bakso code exists in FoodEx2; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for bakso or Indonesian beef meatball as a food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard add-on serving of 3 bakso meatballs, each approximately 25 g, totalling approximately 75 g.

nutrient_source.title: Bakso (beef meatball) — USDA FoodData Central, Beef meatball, cooked; cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asian meatball products
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact bakso entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central under the name 'bakso'. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked beef meatballs (FDC ID 167630, 'Beef, ground, 85% lean / 15% fat, patty, cooked, pan-broiled' as protein/fat anchor) and USDA SR Legacy entry for 'Meatballs, beef, canned' (FDC ID 174609), adjusted for the typical Indonesian bakso formulation which uses a higher proportion of tapioca starch (approximately 20–25%) and lower fat content than Western meatballs. Sodium estimated from typical bakso recipe salt content. Values represent a reasonable central estimate for plain boiled beef bakso per 100 g.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of meat-based and starch-extended meatball products — analogous estimate based on boiled beef meatball with tapioca starch binder
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Indonesian bakso exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 40 (low category) based on: (1) boiled beef meatballs are predominantly protein and fat with moderate tapioca starch content; (2) the University of Sydney GI Database reports GI values of 35–45 for mixed protein-starch meat products; (3) tapioca starch has a GI of approximately 70 but is diluted by the dominant protein-fat matrix, yielding a blended GI in the low range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 6.375) / 100 ≈ 2.6, where carbohydrate per 75 g serving = 8.5 × 0.75 = 6.375 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 480 mg per 100 g; a 3-piece serving (75 g) contributes approximately 360 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit bakso add-on servings and avoid high-sodium broths typically served alongside, as the combined sodium load from meatballs and soup can approach or exceed 600–800 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated 40) and low glycemic load per serving (approximately 2.6) due to dominant protein-fat matrix with moderate tapioca starch
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bakso meatballs have a low glycemic index and low glycemic load per standard 3-piece serving, making them a relatively safe protein-containing add-on for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as noodles or rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content approximately 2.6 g per 100 g and cholesterol approximately 45 mg per 100 g; sodium also elevated
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate bakso intake due to its saturated fat and sodium content, and should prefer versions with leaner beef and lower salt, avoiding high-fat broths to keep total saturated fat and sodium within daily cardiovascular targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bakso is a traditional Indonesian beef meatball made from a mixture of finely ground beef, tapioca starch, garlic, salt, and white pepper, formed into smooth round balls and boiled until cooked through. The texture is firm yet springy due to the tapioca starch binder, and the flavour is savoury and mildly seasoned. Bakso originated in Indonesia and is one of the most popular street foods and hawker dishes across Southeast Asia, widely sold in Singapore at Indonesian-style restaurants and hawker stalls. It is commonly served as an add-on to noodle soups, mie ayam (chicken noodle), or soto, or eaten on its own with chilli sauce and sweet soy sauce. A standard add-on portion of 3 pieces weighs approximately 75 g. Nutritionally, bakso is a moderate-energy food providing approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with a meaningful protein contribution of approximately 14.5 g per 100 g from beef, moderate fat at 6.5 g per 100 g, and a moderate carbohydrate content of approximately 8.5 g per 100 g from the tapioca starch binder. Sodium content is notable at approximately 480 mg per 100 g, which is clinically relevant for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The glycemic index is estimated to be low (approximately 40), making bakso a relatively glycaemia-friendly protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions without high-carbohydrate accompaniments.

confidence_reason: No exact bakso entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or a directly matching USDA FoodData Central entry. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked beef meatball data and adjusted for the typical Indonesian bakso formulation (higher tapioca starch, lower fat than Western meatballs). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework for mixed protein-starch meat products. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation is methodologically sound and the source data is from approved primary databases.
notes: 1. Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is -1 as it is not reliably reported for bakso in the adapted USDA source entries used. 2. Folate (folate_mcg_dfe) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source. 3. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source. 4. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source. 5. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source. 6. Vitamin C (vitamin_c_mg) is 0.0; confirmed zero as beef meatballs contain no vitamin C by composition. 7. The food is listed under cuisine 'Singapore' in the input (field survey data context) but the dish is Indonesian in origin; region_of_origin reflects this dual context. 8. The serving size of 75 g (3 × approximately 25 g balls) is an estimate based on typical bakso ball dimensions; actual weight may vary by restaurant. 9. GI is an analogous estimate; a direct measurement for bakso is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 10. The input food_name 'Additional Bakso 3pcs' has been canonicalised to 'Bakso (Beef Meatballs) 3 pieces' for clarity and LLM retrieval quality.
